card.title: The Monty Hall Problem
card.teaser: Three doors, one car, one ridiculous math puzzle that broke thousands of PhDs in 1990.
card.coverImagePrompt: Three glowing game-show doors on a retro television stage, one cracked open with golden light spilling out, the others dark, velvet curtains, dramatic spotlight, cinematic moody palette

lessons.title: The letter that broke the math world
lessons.body: In 1990, columnist Marilyn vos Savant published a simple puzzle in Parade magazine. Her answer was correct — but over 10,000 readers, including nearly 1,000 PhDs, wrote in to tell her she was wrong. Some letters were furious. The puzzle: you're on a game show, facing three doors.

visual.imagePrompt: A pile of 1990s-era angry letters on a wooden desk, some postmarked from universities, a single newspaper column visible beneath them

lessons.title: The setup
lessons.body: Behind one door: a car. Behind the other two: goats. You pick a door — say, Door 1. The host, Monty, who knows where the car is, opens a different door (say, Door 3) to reveal a goat. He then asks: 'Do you want to switch to Door 2?' Most people say it doesn't matter. It's 50/50, right?

keyTerms.definition: The probability of an event given that another event has already occurred
The set of all possible outcomes in a probability problem

visual.imagePrompt: Three identical numbered doors against a retro teal background, small goat silhouettes faintly visible through two of them

lessons.title: Why switching wins 2/3 of the time
lessons.body: Your first pick had a 1-in-3 chance of being right. That means there was a 2-in-3 chance the car was behind one of the OTHER two doors. Monty hasn't changed those odds — he's just politely removed a losing door from that group. All of that 2/3 probability now concentrates on the one remaining door. Switch, and you win two out of three times.
lessons.funFact: If you scale the puzzle up to 100 doors — you pick one, Monty opens 98 goats, leaving yours and one other — suddenly switching feels obvious. It's the same logic.

visual.imagePrompt: Animated-style diagram showing probability flowing from two doors into one remaining door, arrows and percentages elegantly labeled

lessons.title: Pigeons beat Harvard students
lessons.body: Researchers at Whitman College ran the Monty Hall puzzle on pigeons. Within a few days, the pigeons learned to switch almost every time — maximizing their food rewards. Meanwhile, human students given the same problem kept sticking with their first choice, clinging to a gut instinct the pigeons simply didn't have.

visual.imagePrompt: A pigeon in a Skinner box pecking at one of three small doors, scientific notebook visible in the foreground, soft laboratory lighting

lessons.title: Why your brain hates this
lessons.body: The Monty Hall problem is famous because the correct answer feels wrong. Human intuition is built for small numbers and independent events — not for situations where a knowledgeable host gives you filtered information. The trick isn't in the math; it's in noticing that Monty's choice was never random.

quiz.question: After Monty opens a door revealing a goat, what's your chance of winning if you switch?
quiz.explanation: Your original door still carries its 1/3 probability. The other 2/3 collapses onto the single unopened door — so switching wins two out of three times.

quiz.question: Why does Monty's knowledge matter to the puzzle?
options: It doesn't — the math is the same either way
He deliberately avoids revealing the car, which shifts the probabilities
He sometimes lies about what's behind the doors
He randomly picks a door, just like the contestant
quiz.explanation: Because Monty never opens the car door, his action is informative. A random Monty would make the problem genuinely 50/50.

quiz.question: In the 100-door version, you pick one and Monty opens 98 goats. What are the odds switching wins?
quiz.explanation: Your original pick had a 1/100 chance. All 99/100 of the remaining probability piles onto the one door Monty left closed.

sources.title: Are Birds Smarter Than Mathematicians? Pigeons and the Monty Hall Dilemma
